skill.name: delon-auth-authentication-authorization
skill.description: Guides Angular teams through authentication and authorization patterns with @delon/auth, Firebase Auth, JWT token handling, route guards, permission checks, and @delon/acl integration.
skill.summary: Build @delon/auth login, token, route guard, and permission patterns for Angular applications.
skill.author: 7Spade

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged dynamic imports, Markdown backticks, URLs, Base64 decoding, and weak-crypto patterns. Most pattern hits are false positives in Angular examples or documentation references, but the skill includes auth guidance that could be copied into production with unsafe client-side authorization assumptions.

medium_findings.title: Client-Side Authorization Can Be Bypassed
medium_findings.description: The examples implement role, permission, route, and component checks in the Angular client. These checks are useful for user experience, but they must not be treated as the only authorization control for protected API operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The authorization decisions are visibly performed in client-side Angular code. This is a real security concern if the examples are copied without matching server-side enforcement.

medium_findings.title: JWT Payload Decoded Without Verification Guidance
medium_findings.description: The token parser decodes the JWT payload with Base64 and uses claims to build application auth state. The skill does not show issuer, audience, expiration, or signature verification guidance for trusted server-side authorization decisions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly decodes a JWT payload and reads role-related fields. Firebase SDK token retrieval is legitimate, but the missing verification guidance creates a copy-paste risk.

medium_findings.title: Placeholder Permission Loader Grants Fixed Permissions
medium_findings.description: The permission loading example returns fixed task permissions for every user. This is marked as a placeholder, but a copied implementation could grant unauthorized access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static return value is explicit and grants write access. The comment says it is a placeholder, which lowers malicious-intent confidence but not implementation risk.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Findings Are Angular Lazy Loading
low_findings.description: The dynamic import calls are hardcoded Angular route lazy-loading examples. No user-controlled module path or remote code loading was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each import path is a literal relative Angular component path. This matches normal framework routing rather than script execution abuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fence delimiters. No shell command, Ruby interpolation, or command execution instruction was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown code block markers around TypeScript examples. There is no executable shell context in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Relative API Or Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The HTTP call uses a relative application API path, and the hardcoded URLs are documentation references. No credential exfiltration endpoint or unsolicited external request was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API path is relative, and the external URLs appear only in a References section. I found no evidence of hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections occur on auth descriptions and permission helper lines, not on MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms. No weak cryptographic primitive was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptions, permission array checks, and @delon/auth prose. No actual cryptographic algorithm selection appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Base64 JWT Payload Parsing
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill decodes JWT payload data in the client. This is acceptable for display or local state, but unsafe as a trusted authorization source without server-side token verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Base64 decode is present and semantically tied to auth state. The surrounding Firebase context makes legitimate use possible, so the concern is medium rather than high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Client-Only Role And Permission Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: Role and permission helpers run entirely in the client. Backend APIs must repeat authorization checks using verified identity and permission data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly makes allow or deny decisions in Angular. This is a known limitation of client-side guards, not proof of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Permission Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The placeholder permission loader returns read and write task permissions. It should be replaced with a real permission lookup before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The granted permissions are explicit in the example. The placeholder comment indicates instructional intent, but copied code would be unsafe.

content.value_statement: Angular teams need consistent login, token, and route authorization patterns. This skill provides @delon/auth examples for Firebase login, JWT handling, guards, ACL sync, and permission checks.
actual_capabilities: Configures @delon/auth token headers, login paths, anonymous routes, and refresh timing.
Shows an Angular AuthService with Firebase login, logout, token refresh, and session cleanup.
Creates route guards for authentication, roles, and permission requirements.
Builds a login component with reactive forms, validation, loading state, and error display.
Demonstrates @delon/acl synchronization for role and permission data.
Provides Angular route examples with protected child routes and lazy-loaded components.
limitations: The examples are guidance, not a complete production security architecture.
Client-side guards must be backed by server-side authorization checks.
The permission loading function is a placeholder and must be replaced.
The skill assumes Angular, @delon/auth, @delon/acl, and Firebase Auth.

use_cases.title: Add Login To An Angular Admin App
use_cases.description: Use the skill to create Firebase email login, token storage, logout, and a validated login form.

use_cases.title: Protect Routes By Role Or Permission
use_cases.description: Use the guard patterns to restrict dashboard, admin, and task routes based on auth state and permissions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Delon Auth Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the checklist and examples to align @delon/auth, @delon/acl, and route configuration across a team.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Delon Auth Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to configure @delon/auth in my Angular app with bearer token headers, a login route, and anonymous route exclusions.

prompt_templates.title: Build Firebase Login Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an Angular AuthService for Firebase email login, logout, token refresh, and clean session reset.

prompt_templates.title: Add Route Permission Guards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add auth, role, and permission guards for my Angular routes, and explain where server authorization must also happen.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Auth Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my @delon/auth design for token lifecycle, ACL synchronization, backend authorization, and unsafe client-only assumptions.

output_examples.input: I need login and logout for a Delon Angular app using Firebase Auth.
output: An AuthService plan for Firebase login, token refresh, and logout.
A login component outline with validation and error states.

output_examples.input: I need to protect admin and task routes by permissions.
output: A route guard strategy for authenticated users, roles, and permissions.
Route metadata recommendations for required roles and permission sets.
A warning to enforce the same authorization on backend APIs.

output_examples.input: I want to sync permissions into @delon/acl.
output: An ACL synchronization approach for roles and ability lists.
Guidance to source permissions from trusted backend data.
Checks to keep UI hiding separate from API authorization.

best_practices: Use client-side guards for navigation control, and enforce authorization again on backend APIs.
Load roles and permissions from trusted server data, not hardcoded frontend defaults.
Validate token issuer, audience, expiration, and signature on trusted server paths.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on Angular route guards as the only access control.
Do not ship placeholder permission lists that grant write access.
Do not treat decoded JWT payload fields as trusted without verification.

faq.question: Does this skill implement a complete auth system?
faq.answer: No. It provides Angular and @delon/auth patterns that need project-specific backend validation.

faq.question: Can I use it with Firebase Auth?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use Firebase Auth for email login, logout, and ID token refresh.

faq.question: Does it cover role based access control?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes role helpers, permission helpers, route guards, and @delon/acl synchronization.

faq.question: Are the route guards enough for security?
faq.answer: No. Route guards protect the user interface, but backend APIs must enforce authorization.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated medium?
faq.answer: The scanner found mostly false positives, but the examples include client-only authorization and placeholder permissions.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
